topLineRecommendation.summary: Keep the canonical atomic writer, routed identity, and thin continuity contract; trim or defer Tier 3 routing, reconsolidation, save-time enrichment, and score-heavy quality layers from the default save path.

answeredQuestions.text: Load-bearingness per subsystem (what breaks if removed?)
answeredQuestions.answer: Only canonical routing, atomic promotion/rollback, routed record identity, and thin continuity validation are strictly load-bearing. Most other save-time features are optional or deferable.

answeredQuestions.text: Command substitution feasibility (can a redesigned /memory:save replace the automation?)
answeredQuestions.answer: Yes. A planner-first /memory:save can reuse the existing dry-run/preflight surface, let the AI apply canonical-doc edits, and reserve auto-writer/index refresh behavior for optional follow-up steps.

answeredQuestions.text: Quality gate value (which gates prevent real defects today?)
answeredQuestions.answer: Hard structural and canonical-doc legality checks still prevent malformed saves. Weighted scoring, trigger quotas, and semantic dedup are helpful but oversized for the remaining contract.

answeredQuestions.text: Semantic indexing dependency (save-side vs retrieval-side concerns)
answeredQuestions.answer: Immediate reindexing matters for freshness, but canonical-doc mutation does not depend on it and it can be separated from the write core.

answeredQuestions.text: Graph-metadata refresh necessity (tied to save vs standalone?)
answeredQuestions.answer: Graph-metadata refresh is derived-value and can be run separately because the refresh API derives from canonical docs and preserves manual fields.

answeredQuestions.text: Entity extraction + cross-doc linking value (does it earn its cost?)
answeredQuestions.answer: These features add retrieval-side metadata, but because they are optional and non-blocking they are better deferred to indexing/backfill than kept on the hot save path.
Reconsolidation-on-save is disproportionate because it requires flags, embeddings, and checkpoints, then degrades to warnings instead of guarding correctness.

answeredQuestions.text: Routing-prototypes classifier scope (is the 3-tier design over-fitted?)
answeredQuestions.answer: Yes. The 8-category contract still earns its keep, but the current Tier 2 library size and optional Tier 3 model path are over-fitted for the simplified canonical routing problem.
Trigger normalization still has value, but the save path does not need hard synchronous enforcement when indexing can harmonize lazily.

answeredQuestions.text: Continuity sync behaviour (save flow vs direct Edit by AI)
answeredQuestions.answer: The continuity block is important, but repo governance explicitly allows direct AI edits for continuity-only updates, so universal save-flow ownership is not required.

keyFindings.title: The script wrapper no longer owns legacy memory-file writes
Canonical atomic preparation plus routed identity are the true write core
The router contract remains useful but the classifier stack is oversized
Save-time quality enforcement mixes real guards with ceremonial scoring
Indexing and graph refresh are separable freshness features
Continuity contract matters more than save-flow ownership
Post-insert enrichment is optional by construction
A planner-first /memory:save is achievable without rebuilding the stack

ruledOut.text: Full redesign of the canonical writer
ruledOut.reason: Atomic promotion, rollback, and routed identity are already strong and should be preserved.
The wrapper still enforces explicit target authority and structured-input validation.

ruledOut.text: Keep graph refresh synchronously bound to every save
ruledOut.reason: Graph metadata is derived from canonical docs and has a standalone refresh API.

ruledOut.text: Drop continuity entirely in a minimal flow
ruledOut.reason: Resume and packet-local stop-state still depend on `_memory.continuity`.

generate_context_cli.rationale: Useful for explicit target authority and structured-input normalization, but not the canonical write engine.
risks: Losing explicit CLI target precedence would make operator intent ambiguous.

workflow_orchestrator.rationale: The workflow still refreshes graph metadata and reindexes canonical docs, but no longer owns legacy artifact writes.
risks: If trimmed too far, save-time freshness for graph metadata and index visibility will lag.

canonical_atomic_writer.rationale: Canonical route planning, anchor merge, validation, promotion, rollback, and post-write record creation are the save core.
risks: Removing it would risk corrupted docs and non-atomic failures.

routed_record_identity.rationale: Canonical file path plus anchor-aware identity prevents append-only versioning and dedup from collapsing across targets.
risks: Dropping identity hints would create same-path collisions and lineage errors.

content_router_core.rationale: The save path still needs category, target doc, anchor, and merge-mode planning.
risks: Over-simplifying route planning would misplace durable content.

routing_classifier_stack.rationale: Tier 2 prototype breadth plus optional Tier 3 model calls exceed the now-stable 8-category routing problem.
risks: Trimming too aggressively could increase manual-review cases if Tier 1 rules are not expanded appropriately.

quality_loop.rationale: Auto-fix retries, trigger quotas, and token-budget trimming are heavier than the remaining canonical-doc contract needs.
risks: If removed entirely without a lighter replacement, trigger quality may drift.

save_quality_gate.rationale: Structural and legality checks matter, but weighted content scoring and semantic dedup can be softened or moved out of the default hot path.
risks: Weakening structural rules would allow malformed canonical saves.

semantic_indexing_reindex.rationale: Important for immediate retrieval freshness, but canonical mutation can succeed without it.
risks: Decoupling without a visible follow-up step could leave operators thinking a save is searchable when it is not yet indexed.

graph_metadata_refresh.rationale: Derived packet metadata is useful, but the refresh can run explicitly because it recomputes from canonical docs.
risks: Delayed refresh can briefly stale packet graph surfaces.

thin_continuity_sync.rationale: The continuity schema and markdown upsert path are still central to resume-safe packet state.
risks: Relaxing validation would degrade packet recovery quality.

pe_gating.rationale: Useful for row-level duplicate arbitration and lineage, but not required to write correct canonical docs.
risks: Dropping it without preserving same-path lineage could grow duplicate indexed rows.

reconsolidation_bridge.rationale: Checkpoint-gated reconsolidation and assistive logging add cost without guarding canonical correctness.
risks: Removing it may reduce automatic near-duplicate cleanup for indexed rows.

chunking_orchestrator.rationale: Needed only for oversized content; it should stay as a fallback instead of shaping the default path.
risks: Removing it would leave no safe path for very large saves.

post_insert_enrichment.rationale: Entities, summaries, entity linking, and graph lifecycle are already optional best-effort steps and do not need to block the default save path.
risks: Deferring all enrichment without a background or explicit follow-up path would reduce immediate graph/search richness.
